summary: Gruber recounts a personal anecdote from the September 2014 Apple Watch unveiling event, where he found himself alone in a waiting area with Dr. Mehmet Oz before their respective press briefings. The piece is occasioned by Trump's nomination of Oz to run the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services. Gruber describes Oz as a man of striking celebrity presence but stunning vapidity, who chattered inanely about the Apple Watch prototypes on display as though performing for a nonexistent TV camera. The post uses this firsthand encounter to paint Oz as someone simultaneously accomplished in medicine yet remarkably hollow in person, implicitly questioning his fitness for a major government role.
key_points: Trump nominated Dr. Oz to run CMS, prompting Gruber to share a decade-old personal encounter
At the 2014 Apple Watch unveiling, Gruber was stuck alone with Oz in a waiting area before their respective briefings with Apple executives
Oz's behavior suggested someone perpetually performing for cameras even when none were present, making inane observations about watch bands and colors
Gruber found Oz to be a paradox: a legitimately accomplished surgeon who was staggeringly uninteresting and vapid in person
The anecdote serves as implicit commentary on Oz's fitness for high government office
money_quotes: It's like being in a room with a million dollars in $100 bills stacked in a perfectly-arranged pyramid. No matter how hard you try to direct your attention, your mind keeps popping back to Holy shit, there's a million dollars in cash right there.
He was not talking to me, nor was he, really, talking to himself. It was like he was talking to a TV camera, as though we were being filmed for B-roll footage for his show — but there was no camera.
I came away with the impression that Mehmet Oz was, despite his well-deserved medical renown, preternaturally vapid and preening, and, thus, to me, an incongruous figure.
Simultaneously a brilliant mind in the field of thoracic surgery, and yet dumb as a rock in everyday human interaction.
I spent the first few minutes with him wondering if I should introduce myself. I spent the last few glad I hadn't, because he was so obviously a staggeringly uninteresting and uninterested man.

He seemed genuinely interested in Apple Watch, but not for what Apple Watch actually was or could be, but simply because it was a major new thing, and he was a VIP invitee at the introduction of this major new thing.
key_insight: A firsthand encounter with Dr. Oz at Apple's 2014 event revealed a man of pure celebrity performance — perpetually on camera even when no camera existed — making his nomination to run a major federal agency feel like a punchline.
